quote: I just have a few questions to get started. Is that okay?
stewart_read: The rep asks explicit permission before launching into verify. This is a low-cost micro-commitment that converts Carlene's neutral posture into active consent ('Yes. That's okay.' at 00:27). It's the one deliberate rapport move in the call and it works.
coaching_implication: This is a success signal worth naming in the 1-on-1. The permission-ask is the right instinct. The coaching question is: why did this energy disappear after 00:27? The rest of the call is checkbox execution. Use this moment to show the rep what they're capable of and ask them to carry it through the bill anchor.

stewart_read: Carlene confirms her husband is on the title. The rep acknowledges it ('K.') and moves immediately to utility company. No attempt to include the husband in the appointment. Strategic read: the rep may be planning to raise it at button-up. Reactive read: the rep forgot or deprioritized it. The button-up at 05:11-05:48 never returns to the husband — the strategic read is not supported. This is a reactive miss.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deferring spouse inclusion to the button-up phase as a deliberate sequencing choice.
coaching_implication: The codex is explicit: co-title holders must be on the appointment or the close is structurally at risk. The rep had the information at 00:48 and never used it. In the 1-on-1, replay from 00:48 to the end of button-up and ask: 'Where was the moment to bring the husband in?' The answer is the button-up time-slot question at 05:11 — 'What time works for both of you?' is the move that was available and unused.

stewart_read: Carlene's bill answer is ambiguous — she doesn't know her own number. The rep responds 'Okay' and moves to roof type without confirming the figure. Two gaps compound here: (1) the verify item is incomplete — the actual monthly amount is unknown; (2) the bill anchor, which the codex calls 'the anchor for the entire pitch,' cannot be flipped emotionally if the number isn't confirmed. You cannot say 'that's the number we're working to replace' when you don't have the number.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep planned to extract the exact figure from the bill photo and use it at the appointment — deferring confirmation to the bill-capture step.
coaching_implication: The strategic read (defer to bill photo) is partially defensible, but the rep never circled back to confirm the figure even after receiving the photo at 05:00. In the 1-on-1: 'After you got the photo, what did you know about her bill?' If the rep can't answer, that's the gap. The suggested move: after the photo comes through, read back the kWh usage and say 'So you're paying somewhere around — whatever that exact number is — that's what we're working to replace.' The codex flags this as a universal floor gap: bill captured, not flipped.

quote: Okay. And then do you have your roof is a shingle?
stewart_read: The rep asks roof type (shingle confirmed) but never asks roof age. The codex's verify.roof_type_age item requires both, and roof age over 15 years is a hard DQ trigger or a roofing cross-sell signal. Without the age, the rep cannot assess whether this lead is solar-eligible or should be routed to the roofing team. The gap is invisible on this call but surfaces as a problem at the closer stage if the roof is too old.
coaching_implication: Add one question: 'And roughly how old is that roof?' It takes five seconds and closes a hard-DQ risk. If Carlene says 'I don't know,' the rep notes it for the closer. If she says '20 years,' the rep has a roofing cross-sell signal (cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned). Either way, the information is worth having. This is a verify discipline gap, not a persuasion gap.

quote: That's how we know how much we can save you.
stewart_read: The rep uses the bill-photo request as a data-collection step and attaches a generic savings promise ('that's how we know how much we can save you'). This is the codex's documented failure pattern: 'ask + acknowledge + move on' instead of 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The flip was available — Carlene had just said she's paying 'four or five something' a month. The emotional alignment-against-utility move (codex: objection_inversion) was unused.
coaching_implication: The codex notes zero instances of the flip across the corpus. This call is another data point. In the 1-on-1, show the rep what the flip looks like without fabricating a number: 'Whatever that exact figure is — four hundred, five hundred — that's money going to the utility every month. The design we build is going to show you what it looks like to stop paying that.' The number Carlene gives is the leverage; the rep treated it as a form field.

quote: If they come in first, what would what would happen? What would they do if they come in first? Just anything different from on the phone?
stewart_read: Carlene's question about in-person was created by the rep's offer at 03:05. The rep's answer ('No. It's all the same.') is brief and unelaborated — Carlene accepted it without complaint, so no damage was done. But the exchange consumed time and introduced uncertainty that the codex's standard phone-appointment protocol would have avoided entirely. This is a codex gap as much as a rep gap: the script doesn't explicitly prohibit in-person offers, but the button-up protocol doesn't include them.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep offered in-person deliberately to give Carlene a sense of control over the appointment format, increasing commitment.
coaching_implication: The strategic read (choice = commitment) is plausible but not supported by any follow-through — the rep didn't use the choice moment to deepen buy-in. Until the codex is updated with Kenny's guidance on in-person offers, coach the rep to default to phone-only framing per button_up.phone_appointment_specified. If in-person is a legitimate option at Ion, this needs a protocol entry.

quote: I'll text you after this to remind you. And then when they call you, all they ask is that you're not driving and you can access your email. Okay?
stewart_read: The button-up is missing two required elements: (1) the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake (button_up.is_that_fair_handshake), which the codex identifies as predictive of show rate; and (2) the rep says 'email' where the codex specifies 'computer' — a minor drift but worth noting. The text-followup promise is present. The husband is still not included. The appointment is booked but the verbal commitment ritual that anchors show rate was skipped.
coaching_implication: Two-line fix in the 1-on-1: after 'you can access your email,' add 'Is that fair?' and pause for the yes. Then: 'And will your husband be available at 11 as well?' Both lines take under 10 seconds and materially change the appointment's durability. The 'is that fair?' handshake is the single highest-leverage button-up element the rep skipped.

quote: Yeah. Do eleven. I have a 12:30 appoint — 02:00 appointment. So eleven is okay.
stewart_read: Carlene volunteers her schedule to protect the 11 AM slot — she's already planning around the appointment. This is a behavioral commitment signal, not just verbal agreement. The rep receives it and confirms the time but doesn't anchor on it ('You're already making room for this — that tells me you're serious about getting this looked at'). The signal also reopens the husband question: if Carlene has a 12:30, does the husband? The rep had a natural door to ask 'And will your husband be free at 11 as well?' and didn't take it.
coaching_implication: Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to show the rep what an enthusiasm signal looks like. Carlene is telling you she wants this appointment. The coaching question: 'What could you have done with that?' The answer is two moves — anchor the commitment ('you're already protecting that time') and use it to bring the husband in ('does he have the same window?'). Neither requires fabricating anything; both are available from what Carlene said.
